summary:
The Treaty of Shimonoseki, signed on 17 April 1895, ended the [[event:2550|First Sino-Japanese War]] and imposed far-reaching terms on China that reflected the completeness of Japan's military victory. It represented a dramatic reversal of the traditional East Asian order in which China had long been the dominant cultural and political power.

The treaty's main provisions included Chinese recognition of Korean independence — effectively ending the tribute relationship that had given China influence over the peninsula — the cession of Taiwan, the Pescadores Islands, and the Liaodong Peninsula to Japan, and the payment of a massive indemnity of 200 million taels of silver. China also opened additional ports to Japanese trade and commerce, extending the treaty port system established by the earlier unequal treaties with Western powers.

The acquisition of the Liaodong Peninsula was reversed almost immediately by the Triple Intervention of Russia, France, and Germany, who pressured Japan to return the territory to China in exchange for an additional 30 million taels. Russia then proceeded to lease the very same Liaodong Peninsula for itself two years later — a humiliation that Japanese public opinion never forgot and that contributed directly to the decision to fight the [[event:2561|Russo-Japanese War]] in 1904.

For China, the treaty was a catastrophic blow. The loss to Japan — a country that China had culturally influenced for centuries — was more psychologically devastating than the earlier defeats to Western powers. The treaty accelerated the internal debate between the [[event:2625|Self-Strengthening Movement's]] cautious modernization and the more radical reforms demanded by the [[event:2587|Hundred Days' Reform]] movement of 1898. Taiwan remained under Japanese rule until Japan's defeat in 1945.
